chapters.title: How to harness information overload
chapters.text:
Nowadays, information isn't an object or asset but a style of living. Reaching for our smartphone has become our first morning task, while watching videos is one of the most popular ways to relax. We don't even realize how much data we absorb. Yet scientists have calculated it — 34 gigabytes per day. Impressive, isn't it?

This informational deluge aims to make us smarter. Indeed, the answers to our questions are only one click away, which is enormously convenient. However, what we have read or watched usually gets lost in the labyrinths of our memory. As a result of consuming tons of information, we often feel ill-equipped. To change this, we should reconsider our approach to information.

Increase your productivity by making the most of what you have already learned.

Since school, we have relied on the "disposable knowledge" model. It means we learn something, use it during tests, and happily forget it. Yet, this model doesn't suit our fast-paced world. These days, our past knowledge can be useful at any minute. How can we prepare ourselves for these situations? An effective note-taking system is a must.

Stick around if you strive to boost your knowledge management skills and ride the information wave.

chapters.title: An extension for our memory
chapters.text:
The trickiest part of dealing with information lies in its timing: we often don't require it the moment we obtain it. In theory, this allows us to gather necessary data in advance, which seems beneficial.

However, practically, it often results in many notes and the subsequent difficulty in locating them. According to International Data Corporation research, employees spend a quarter of their workday searching for scribbles and misplaced files. So, how can we improve our knowledge management?

A fundamental step is rethinking the note-taking process. It's more than just recording ideas as fast as possible; we should also consider the addressee — our future selves. At that time, we will have other tasks on our plates, and the last thing we need is to reread and decode what we have collected.

Thus, Forte suggests a system called a Second Brain. It's a digital space, such as a folder on a laptop or note-taking app, where we will save the most critical insights and allow them to grow into masterpieces. So, what precisely can a Second Brain do?

• Make room for thinking: With an organized note system, we can channel the energy we spend on remembering data into processing it and creating something new.
• Elevate self-esteem: Seeing how a Second Brain fills with more and more insights gives us the confidence to act on our knowledge.
• Boost mindfulness: Taking notes is an excellent self-reflection tool. The more we write down, the more patterns we detect, which nurtures self-understanding.
• Give ideas a visible form: Unlike thoughts, we can categorize and refine our notes.
• Spark creativity: Our minds are generators of associations. When we group insights from business meetings and philosophers' quotes, new perspectives on the issue are unavoidable.
• Create a safety net: A major thinking pitfall is recency bias. While making essential decisions or simply brainstorming, we rely on what we have read or listened to lately. A well-organized system of notes assists us in seeing the broader spectrum of opportunities.

Gamify your note-taking, considering it an exploration with small daily goals.

Now, it's time to explore the four steps (CODE) that allow us to create a Second Brain.

chapters.title: Which data should we note?
chapters.text:
Each Second Brain starts with capturing information. Balance is crucial: we shouldn't record every new idea nor naively hope that insights will automatically fix in our minds; these are two extremes. To be on the safe side, we need some filters for ideas. But which?

Let's contemplate how brilliant scientist Richard Feynman evaluated his insights. Being interested in many things simultaneously, he always had a list of 12 problems that drove his curiosity. Each time he came across an interesting idea, he applied it to these problems, and sometimes an inspiring moment appeared.

This technique helped him in many spheres. For instance, while visiting the university's cafeteria, he observed students throwing a plate. What immediately caught his attention was how uncoordinated its spin and wobble were. He wrote this insight down and developed it into a discovery in quantum electrodynamics, which later received a Nobel prize.

Noticing gaps in knowledge can be as valuable as eureka moments.

We may also benefit from Feynman's technique; the trick is to choose problems that matter to us. How can we do this? Recalling our previous interests is one way. For example, Forte's passion for "How to organize processes better" stems from childhood. Instead of focusing on immediately building something from LEGO blocks, he spent hours sorting them out by size or color.

Also, we can track what we think about the most. As a result, our lists may include lifelong questions, such as "How can I make my life more balanced?" or we can concentrate on practical issues, such as "Which additional source of income should I choose?"

Apart from insights that address our 12 issues, we should capture the following data:

• Inspirational: This is a very individual experience, so using a set of quotes from the Internet won't do the trick. Having personal notes that motivate, move, and incite us will be our secret weapon in times of boredom or exhaustion. Forte, for instance, gathers messages from his customers.
• Useful: Statistics for our reports, facts to argue our viewpoints, or any other helpful pieces of data fall into this category.
• Personal: Ideas based on our experiences are invaluable as we won't find them in books or other sources.
• Surprising: These insights are paramount, being simultaneously rare and influential. If something evokes this feeling, it broadens our patterns of thinking, which can be game-changing.

chapters.title: The recipe for easy-to-use notes
chapters.text:
Recording insights is crucial, and so is their organization. As a rule, people tend to copy large fragments from sources and sort them out immediately between subfolders of the Second Brain. Unfortunately, this strategy is not very effective.

Firstly, notes should include less than 10% of the source information, making them easier to process and recall. If we need additional details, we can add a link or book title. Otherwise, upon seeing a long text, our future selves will put this note away without reading it, wasting our diligent work.

Keeping records and notes short will help you remember more.

Secondly, folders are indispensable — we will dive into this in a few seconds — but the moderation stage is critical. It means collecting all notes in one general folder and then classifying them once a week. This approach has a few advantages:

• It makes capturing easier: Recording ideas and immediately sorting them out requires much time and effort. It can turn note-taking into a feat, so we do it less often. Thus, we should simplify the process; separating, collecting, and organizing will do the trick.
• It lets ideas simmer: Looking through our notes after some time, we will filter the useful ones. Let's gather only the insights that stood the test of time.

After this stage, we can finally categorize our notes. But what folders should we create? Forte has tried several systems and concluded that topic-based ones don't work. They are too broad, complicating the search. For example, in "Psychology," we would have tips for our personal lives, diagrams to include in our blogs, and exciting tidbits. Instead, we can arrange notes according to their actionability using Forte's PARA model, which consists of four categories:

• Projects: Its subfolders relate to short-term tasks, like making a presentation, renovating the kitchen, and organizing a birthday party.
• Areas: Here, we save notes for long-term duties or needs, such as work responsibilities, domestic chores, self-care, and finances.
• Resources: These subfolders preserve insights about our hobbies. Also, it's a space to collect tips for our plans, such as a trip to New Zealand.
• Archives: We can put notes here after finishing the project or losing interest in a topic.

chapters.title: The power of condensing
chapters.text:
After the first and second steps, we will have many tidbits in our folders. Although they aren't long-reads, we should distill their essence to operate them. Francis Ford Coppola, a famous director, has a method that exemplifies this point.

While working on The Godfather, he first selected the episodes that resonated with him. Then, he transformed them into scenes, clarified the details, and added a one-sentence summary, which made rearranging episodes much easier. Thus, the third-best American movie of all time rests not only on inspiration but also on research and organizing systems.

Talent needs to be channeled and developed in order to become something more than a momentary spark. ~ Tiago Forte

We can also use this technique to our advantage, applying the Progressive Summarization method. The essential caveat is that we should do it only if we are ready to turn our notes into something more significant. Our purpose serves as an effective filter for information. For example, when Forte comes up with a topic for his new blog, he selects relevant notes and condenses them.

Progressive Summarization comprises a few steps:

1. Rereading the notes and bolding the most valuable passages
2. Highlighting the critical points in chosen parts
3. Creating a bullet list with cornerstone ideas in case notes are extra long
4. Adding tags if necessary

The main advantage of this method is the zooming option. If we reorganize our notes and must quickly remind ourselves of their essence, we can read lists of highlights. If we need more details, we can scan bold passages. It's also a tool to be on the safe side because we can always change formatting if our priorities alter.

Return to your summarized note in a week and see if you can grasp its idea in 30 seconds. If so, you've done a great job.

But let's keep distilling simple. It is unnecessary to weigh some phrases' pros and cons before highlighting them. Instead, relying on what resonates with us is the best option. Why? It's the language in which our intuition speaks to us. Our gut feelings know more about our interests and where they will lead us; they excel in the helicopter view. So, we should let them be our guiding stars.

Did you know? George Lucas convinced his mentor, Francis Ford Coppola, to work on The Godfather.

chapters.title: How notes become erudition
chapters.text:
The capture, organize, and distill stages work with information, while the express phase turns it into knowledge. The only possible way to do it is by applying the collected tidbits in practice. Social media posts, reports, or books — the project size doesn't matter; the key is to sharpen our perspectives and skills.

What should we start with? Nothing extraordinary: breaking tasks into smaller chunks will make them less daunting. Doing something step-by-step will allow us to ask for feedback at each stage. And when critical remarks relate to a small part, not the whole project, it will be easier — physically and mentally — to act on them.

Instead of achievements, we should praise preparation. Victories are just its echo.

Let's suppose we must organize a conference. A typical bullet list will entail many tasks, like an event website and a list of potential speakers. We can perceive them as subfolders of a new project and start to fill them with notes we have. There are a few methods to select suitable ones:

• Search: This is the best option if our tasks have unique keywords that we can easily find.
• Browse: Manual navigation will provide helpful starting points when we are unsure what we are looking for.
• Rely on tags: They shouldn't be the backbone of our classifications, as adding them to each insight is exhausting and often unnecessary. Instead, we can use them in notes that contain valuable information for different spheres. It's also helpful to use tags as mini-summaries. For instance, if a note answers a question we hear many times, we can apply the "FAQ" tag.
• Find by chance: We should always leave room for unexpected discoveries made during scanning notes on five or six related topics. Looking through images can also be a source of inspiration. Infographics, diagrams, and schemes facilitate understanding the data and forming new connections.

Having revised this material, we'll make the beginning — the most challenging part of our project — much easier.

The only way to know whether you’re getting the good stuff is to try putting it to use in real life. ~ Tiago Forte

chapters.title: Habits of a knowledge manager
chapters.text:
Time constraints pressure us to optimize our workflow constantly. How can we do more in less time? A telling example comes from restaurant kitchens. Chefs can't afford to stop the cooking process for cleaning; at the same time, the latter is inevitable. So, they add some habits to their cooking routine that will keep the workplace clean, like wiping appliances after using them or reserving a place for mixing spoons.

Our knowledge management also needs rules to work smoothly, and here are some of them:

• Building bridges: Ernest Hemingway finished his daily writing by planning the plot line for the following period. Similarly, we should also build bridges by leaving some time to outline our next steps. This way, instead of rebooting our brains daily, we will quickly catch where we left off.
• Controlling scope: Sometimes, our bullet lists get so long that we become anxious. How can we do it all? Instead of giving ourselves more time, we should decide whether all tasks are essential. For instance, let's imagine that our presentation is almost finished, except for one point, which needs more research. Instead of burning the candle at both ends, we can skip this idea and develop it in our next presentation. It doesn't mean we delete all we have collected for it; instead, we make it the seed of a new project.
• Alternating capturing and distilling: It's impossible to collect all the necessary information immediately. That's because we only see what we have missed while analyzing what we have gathered.
• Organizing systematically: We shouldn't postpone managing our notes until we have more time. Let's be honest: this way, we will wait for ages. Therefore, we should use each spare minute while commuting or standing in a queue. Spending less time on notes but checking in regularly will have lasting effects.
• Reviewing tasks: We adjust to new challenges; a Second Brain must do the same. Its structure should reflect our priorities. For example, there is no need to have a folder called "Kayaking" if we are no longer passionate about it.

Start creating earlier. This way, you detect your blind spots and search for the data you need.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
Informational deluge prompts us to spend more time online to avoid missing something important. But we don't need to know everything; we should only follow our own path. Some may ask, "How do I find it?" Observing what becomes the focus of our attention may reveal it. Collecting our insights in one place, we will soon see how some folders quickly get packed with ideas. They indicate the direction to head for.

A Second Brain is more than just a tool to store our discovered findings effectively. It's a method to make our lives more meaningful. Firstly, with its help, we will have more free time to follow our aspirations and create deeper relationships. Secondly, whenever we record what resonates with us, we look in the mirror, reflecting the part of us we hardly knew before. The ideas we capture are already living inside us.

There is a notion — "Polanyi's Paradox," named after Hungarian philosopher Michael Polanyi, who discovered that human capacities and knowledge are beyond our understanding. We don't know all our possibilities. So, instead of limiting ourselves to some hobby or occupation, we should strive to reveal new levels of our personality. That's why Forte advocates following what excites us. A Second Brain will help handle the rest.

Try this

Your brain is for having ideas, not holding them. Follow these steps to turn your notes into a creativity engine:

• Determine your way of organizing data: Watch Forte's video "Pick a Notes App: Your Notetaking Style" to find your notetaking archetype.
• Write your notes as tweets: A 280-character limitation will help you cut through fluff and retain only the essence.
• Create smart connections: If a note reminds you of another, connect them, turning scattered information into a knowledge network.
• Apply emojis: Replace boring bullet points in lists with emojis representing emotions, importance, or urgency to summarize your notes better.
• Build a habit, not just a system: Dedicate five minutes daily to maintaining your Second Brain. The more you use it, the more valuable it becomes.

Your best ideas aren't lost — they're waiting to be rediscovered. Start curating them today!

Read it till the end?
